Why not use ultrasonic cleaner instead of blender, which makes fake cavitation bubbles?
Ultrasonic cleaner makes real cavitation bubbles; blender, electric toothbrush, etc, makes fake cavitation bubbles.

author: @insightfool
text: Ok, so I am a little confused about the destinction between scintering and capping this case.  If I want to scinter particles together with the flash will this also allow the gelatin to remain and thus prevent the oxidation?  Or will scintering itself prevent oxidation because the particles are together (regardless of capping)?  Trying to figure out a way to get a premanent copper trace that wont oxidize.  It's not clear to me in your methods, where the "fixer" stage is.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: scintering and capping are very different mate - scintering is a heat process to fuse - capping is covering by a protective coating

replies.author: @insightfool
replies.text: Right.  I understand that distinction.  What I am not clear about is whether the flash scintering takes care of preventing oxidation?  I'm thinking not, but that was a step you didn't cover.  So there must be some extra step where you coat your work after you flash scinter it.

author: @chaitanyabhave2092
text: I've found that adding ammonium hydroxide, in quantities sufficient to complex the copper sulfate, not only allows the reduction to proceed at a faster rate(no heating, very little stirring required), but also significantly improves the yield of the reaction. At the end of the reduction process, all that remains is colorless water(with dissolved salts, naturally, but none of the blue/green tinge that is characteristic of the reduction carried out without ammonia). Although the copper particles still love to oxidize. I was wondering if it would be possible to coat the nano particles with a layer of tin, in the same fashion that copper tracks of PCBs are tinned. It would make for a much stabler copper ink, I think.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chaitanya Bhave the key thing is particle size mate - the reduction speed isn't so important - well only in so much as it effects size. That is going to be a function of starting particle size and speed of the homogeniser as well as concentration. I am guessing a chelating agent would have a big impact too.

replies.author: @chaitanyabhave2092
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith in that reaction ammonia forms a complex salt copper ammonium sulphate, which has a significantly lower reduction potential than just copper. This both improves reaction kinetics, and allows the use of weaker reducing reagents. As for size, chelating agents and low concentrations are probably the best bet. But the easy oxidation of copper is what makes it not practical in my opinion. Flashing is great for industrial purposes, but not so much as a paint and go type of ink.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chaitanya Bhave fair enough

replies.author: @chaitanyabhave2092
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith I've even tried coating them with amorphous carbon...doesn't do much. I guess flash sintering is the only viable option.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chaitanya Bhave i guess

author: @CPedersen2012
text:
Regarding xenon scinterning:
Many profesional (and show type flashes) have flash tubes that are coated to filter out a variety of frequencies.  
They can often be spec'd with either coated or uncoated tubes.  Scintering happens much faster with the uncoated tubes.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good to know - cheers mate

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
a thought I had was, about making zinc nano particles, since you can make zinc sulfate just as easily as making copper sulfate. any info on this? I know copper gets displaced by zinc in a sulfate solution due to it having a higher affinity to bond to the sulfate than copper has, so I guess the trick is finding an ascorbic acid specifically with a metal that is a higher affinity so they can swap places the same way the copper is swapping places,

any idea as to which type of ascorbic acid you used? (magnesium, calcium, potassium)?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i used L-ascorbic acid

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith hmm ok so I wonder if it can be reused the same way that the sulfuric acid can by dissolving a weaker bonding metal in it then switching out to a stronger bonded metal (from the sulfate I am guessing) or is it bonding to the sulfur? any links on what is actually going on here in the solution?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Thomas Anderson
it's just a replacement reaction mate - the reactivity series will help

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
 heh is it odd that I found that while investigating more on sulfuric acid about an hour or less before you posted that? lol (found "that" as in a chart of the reactive series)

author: @joeglory425
text: There's research showing that a thin coating of graphene over copper greatly increases the copper's resistance to corrosion.  I've added graphene in a water/acetone solution to copper nanoparticles and the mixture does clear up which tells me the graphene doesn't stay in solution.  As to whether it is coating the nanoparticles I can't say but it's a reasonable speculation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is - but I have also read that the corrosion protection is not for real. It depends what papers you read on this one i'm afraid

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: It's too bad about the ambiguity of corrosion resistance.  However, the ability of graphene to combine with copper is interesting in itself and worth considering.  I want to do some more experimenting before saying more. This may take me awhile.  My copper sulfate is highly contaminated and I need to clean it up.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Joseph Richardson
it definitely is mate

author: @algirdaslazauskas2733
text: Preferably, the method you demonstrated with copper chloride in microwave is of practical use. I have modified it a little… (the video can be found at Synhesis of copper nanoparticles) The process is now more productive and visibly controllable. I have managed to make spherical (image can be found at http://science.e-business.lt/services) copper nanoparticles with controllable diameter. Next, lets try some simple route to synthesize triangular shape copper nanoparticles! Many thanks for inspiration and best regards. A.L.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Very interesting - i suspect playing around with pH and solvent is going to have a big effect on morphology

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was thinking some more about your experiments and the more I think of it the more I am convinced that changing the pH with sodium hydroxide and adding variable amounts of PVP are going to have dramatic effects - the main reason for thinking this is i was reading a couple of papers that discussed exactly that
